entries.title: People Are Getting Off to Porn About ICE Raids
entries.summary: MAGA-coded adult content playing off immigration crackdowns, white supremacy, and obeying the “natural order” is finding rabid audiences on X.
entries.content:
On July 8, X account @superioritymale posted an update about ICE’s ongoing crackdown on immigrants, describing the conditions being thrust upon detainees.

“Over 68,000 held in ICE detention centers around the United States, many of whom lack access to food, water, and any means to reach contact with their loved ones,” reads the post, which also includes a video. Only, the video wasn’t news footage from inside a detention center. It was a 14-second clip of a white man masturbating in a ski mask.

“Let me serve that cock massa,” replied @FreakedOut222.

Accounts like @superioritymale belong to a niche porn sphere on X where users project, and find sexual pleasure in, pro-MAGA messaging. Over the past two years under President Donald Trump’s second term, they appear to have become even more prevalent on the social media platform.

WIRED identified a dozen accounts, varying in reach and influence—some had tens of thousands of followers—but they all share a similar theme: the fantasy of white male domination. They promote the aesthetic of being a white god or alpha, post about “the natural order” and the “biological truth” of white supremacy, while encouraging followers to claim their “birthright” as the ruling species.

The tone in many of the posts mirrors the extreme rhetoric often championed by the White House. In January, @malesupremacy13, whose bio reads “nazi-fascist” and “war is porn,” posted a list of “Things I jerk off to,” which included “Bombings of Gaza, LGBT rights being denied, gender inequality, [and] environment/plastic pollution.” In one video from last October, clips of white men engaging in various acts of straight and gay sex flicker like a highlight reel as Bruce Springsteen’s “Born in the USA” plays, and a giant text overlay in red, white, and blue reads: “JOIN ICE TODAY.” The X post, which implored followers to “HELP YOUR COUNTRY GET RID OF ILLEGAL CRIMINALS TODAY BRO,” included a link to ice.gov. (Springsteen has been one of Trump’s most vocal critics since entering office; while on tour in 2025 the singer said America was “in the hands of a corrupt, incompetent, and treasonous administration.”)

The White House and the Department of Homeland Security did not respond to requests for comment.

“MAGA porn fulfills that desire for the masculine and the powerful,” says Josh, a 25-year-old Columbus, Ohio, resident who is a fan of these accounts. Josh, who is bisexual, says he’s been a Trump voter since 2020. This type of content is an outlet to deal with the “the strict requirements of performative liberalism” from some gay men in the community who pretend to care about issues that don’t matter to them, he adds. “You wouldn't understand the amount of messages I get from guys telling me they've had enough of putting on a liberal facade. People want to indulge in something a bit naughty and, you know, maybe they can be convinced to vote MAGA.” (Given the explicit nature of the trend and citing professional concerns, Josh asked to be identified by first name only.)

Pro-MAGA porn, which is a plentiful universe of white nationalist beliefs, overlaps with other niche porn subcultures that have helped it to spread more widely, including gooning and financial domination, the BDSM kink where submissives voluntarily give money to their dom, who are sometimes referred to as “cash masters.” On the account @MagaGodJ, messages like “Racism is an all American Value” and others using slurs for gay men are posted over what appear to be AI images of shirtless bodybuilders flexing who are meant to be worshipped and obeyed.

@MagaGodJ blocked my personal account after WIRED made a request for comment.

In a video from July 10, OnlyFans creator MasterJoe wears a red MAGA hat while kicking and taunting one of his submissives, who is white and dressed in a “Harris-Walz 24” T-shirt. “Dumb fucking liberal,” MasterJoe says in the video, which he promoted on X as “SLAVE BEATING VIDEO 8 MIN LONG 50% OFF TODAY ONLY. ($50).”

Several of the accounts promoting MAGA porn also post about incest, fetishize misogyny, and attempt to normalize rape. The pages are often tagged with telltale signs such #ILBAB (I love being a boy) and #BWC (big white cock) in their bios, with some referring to masturbation as “h8 b8ting.”

Others poke fun at the idea of women having no autonomy over their body and the end of DEI. “Just fired the last black on our team. She begged to keep her job, talking about how she has kids to feed. See you at the McDonalds drive thru bitch,” one post reads. Attached is a video depicting a man climaxing in his work clothes. “Black lives only matter when they suck white cock,” says another.

X did not respond to a request for comment.

But perhaps the emergence of such accounts isn’t entirely surprising. After ownership changes in 2022, X embraced a more laissez-faire approach to moderation, becoming a hub for conservative and right-wing commentators. Adult creators who rely heavily on X for promotion suddenly found themselves operating in a feed where political discourse, and particularly conservative media, was much more prominent.

One editor of a gooner music video page on X tells WIRED he gets requests from MAGA porn creators to have their work featured in his widely shared clips. “You’d see [these pages] once upon a time, and they were irrelevant. They’d have like 10 followers. Now they have much bigger audiences,” he says. “People are always asking me to make a MAGA edit and I always tell them no, because I don’t want any of that mess over here. I just think those people are fucking morons.” (The editor asked that WIRED not publish his name because he doesn’t want to be associated with the trend.)

While eroticized racism and dom/sub relationships have a long history in the adult entertainment industry, the framing around them online has been updated to reflect current pro-MAGA messaging, much of it made all the more explicit for our pornified internet era.

According to Whitney Strub, an associate professor at Rutgers University-Newark and author of Perversion for Profit: The Politics of Pornography and the Rise of the New Right, Trump’s MAGA movement has always depended on white-nationalist politics that respond to racial fears with displays of masculine strength.

“Fascist sexual politics tend toward the sadomasochistic—the dominance of the master race combined with the submissiveness to the authoritarian and the cuckold fantasies that are rarely far from the surface of conspiracy theories,” he says, citing the alt-right’s Great Replacement theory, which argues that falling white birth rates combined with mass immigration from non-white countries will eventually erase the so-called native white population.

Strub adds that the cruelty on display echoes the violent sexual catharsis that is typical in Nazi and white-nationalist propaganda. “These MAGA sex accounts strike me as the next stage of those trajectories, filtered through the ironypilled sensibilities of Something Awful, 4chan, and groyper meme culture.”

To Josh, the appeal of MAGA-themed porn is pretty simple.

“We value free speech, so we don’t care what your opinions or kinks are as long as they aren’t liberal,” he says. “Patriotism is sexy. Why wouldn’t I be attracted to hot guys who share my views?”

entries.title: Google Turns a Selfie Video Into Your Account’s Spare Key
entries.summary: The next time you’re locked out of your Google account, you can use your face as part of the account recovery process.
entries.content:
Google has a new way for users to sign in to their accounts: a selfie video.

If you’ve ever lost your device and gotten locked out of your Google account, you know it can feel like a desperate, Kafkaesque process to recover access and get back in—especially for people who run their digital lives, from email messages to calendar appointments, on Google software. This new sign-in option is an additional way to unlock your Google account, like a spare key hidden in the bushes.

“We always recommend that you set up more than one option,” says Claire Forszt, a product manager at Google who focuses on identity and engagement. “Selfie is just the newest option in that list available for users. It's designed to help specifically in these vulnerable scenarios around not having access to the device where your passkey might be.” Additional sign-in options include using recovery contacts and backup codes.

I was able to set up my selfie video in less than five minutes, and the process was straightforward. Open your Google account and choose the Security & sign-in tab. Then scroll down to the How you sign in to Google section. If the selfie sign-in is currently available for your account, you’ll see it as a new option at the bottom. Google is in the process of rolling this out globally to most accounts.

I recorded my selfie video on an iPhone, and Google accepted it on my first attempt. It recommends keeping your face visible and eyes pointed at the camera in standard lighting. It also recommends that you’re the only face seen in the recording; even a family portrait hanging behind you is a no-go. As prompted by Google, I lifted my chin slowly and stared at the ceiling for a second before bringing my head back to the center. Then it asked me to look directly at the camera for one last capture. Finally, it verified the selfie video and stored it under Security Settings.

Google says it’s safeguarding users against deepfake attacks with tools like liveness detection. “There might be instances where passing a selfie video alone may not always be sufficient to get you back into your account if we suspect something risky going on,” Forszt says. “We evaluate the overall risk based on many factors.”

If you decide you want to enable this option, it's essential to set it up before you need it. “You can’t add a selfie video while you’re locked out of your account or in the account recovery process,” reads Google’s support page for the feature.

Google gives users an option during the selfie video creation process to decide whether to opt in to that recording being used as training data. “Allow Google to use this and any previously provided selfie videos and related data to develop and improve our facial recognition, age estimation, and other verification methods that use your physical features or movement, across Google services,” reads the description next to an optional checkbox. Users can go into their privacy settings later to adjust this setting, labeled Improve Google services, if they change their mind.

This selfie video process may feel familiar to Google power users who have experimented with other recently released features. Google launched an AI avatar tool earlier this year in its Gemini app where users can create a digital clone of their likeness and insert it into AI videos. I tried this AI avatar feature when it first dropped and was stunned by how photorealistic and natural the generations of me were.

While Google’s approach to these selfie features gives users the choice to opt in, other tech companies have taken a different path with recent tools that involve your likeness and generative AI. Earlier in July, Meta faced harsh blowback from Instagram users when it automatically opted adults into their images being available for AI remixes by anyone, unless they dug around in the settings to opt out. The backlash was so severe that Meta fully deleted the feature three days after its launch.

While some readers will, understandably, still be uncomfortable with this new sign-in method capturing and storing their likeness, I’m glad the feature is opt-in and will likely leave it enabled for my personal account. As someone who gets locked out of their accounts more than they’d like to admit, swiping my face card the next time I’m desperately attempting to claw my way back in will feel like a much-needed relief.

entries.title: Banning Social Media for Kids Is a Bad Idea
entries.summary: The risks are real. But banning children from the internet could create new problems without solving the ones that really matter.
entries.content:
It is clear that social media affects mental health and poses a danger to children and adolescents. One study showed it took just 40 minutes of browsing for a platform in Mexico to recommend content to a teenage girl that contains references to suicide.

What’s most concerning is that these aren’t isolated glitches but rather a result of how algorithms are designed to hook users and keep them on the platforms longer. With infinite scrolling and the instant gratification it provides, social media can become as addictive as cigarettes.

These factors, coupled with concerns about screen time and other worries, have led many countries to enact bans or restrictions on social media for kids. Last year, Australia became the first country to try to keep people under 16 off the apps. In June, the United Arab Emirates enacted a measure locking youth under 15 out of platforms like TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, Facebook, and X.

Just last week, the UK revealed a plan to institute a midnight social media curfew next spring for 16- and 17-year-olds—one they can opt out of. Prime Minister Keir Starmer said in June that he hoped to pass a more stringent ban on social media for under-16s “before Christmas.” Starmer formally resigned earlier this week, but the legislation is likely to proceed. On Tuesday, lawmakers in France passed a bill banning social media for anyone under 15. President Emmanuel Macron is expected to sign it into law.

However, something is happening that, at first glance, seems contradictory. Many of the organizations that have spent years researching these risks and defending children’s rights are not supporting such bans. On the contrary, they warn that disconnecting children from these spaces might address the symptoms but leave the root causes of the problem intact—and could even create new risks.

Australia’s measure was hailed internationally and held up as a model. Six months after its implementation, however, seven out of 10 parents reported that their children still have accounts on Instagram, TikTok, or Snapchat. This is not surprising. Not only because adolescence is typically a time of rebellion but also because it is natural to resist being excluded from spaces where a large part of their social life takes place and where the people they’ve grown up with are hanging out.

Social media platforms are much more than just entertainment for young people, especially for those who grew up during the Covid-19 pandemic. Comparing them to cigarettes ignores the fact that they also serve important functions. They are spaces for socializing, learning, and accessing information that is often unavailable elsewhere, especially in places where educational gaps and disparities in access to public services persist. Generally speaking, how many times has Reddit saved us with an answer we couldn’t find in a textbook, at school, or at a public institution? And how many times has a YouTube tutorial or a thread on X taught us something that no one else explained to us?

Furthermore, social media can provide a sense of community and a space for self-expression to those who don’t always find it outside the internet. For teenagers who belong to historically marginalized groups—such as LGBTQ+ people, people of color, or people with disabilities—these spaces can also offer an opportunity to explore and build their identities with greater freedom, find role models, and feel less alone.

Precisely because social media is much more than just entertainment, it is worth asking whether some solutions might simply replace one harm with another. Age verification, for example, involves collecting highly sensitive data, making platforms more attractive to cybercriminals and putting the privacy and safety of minors at risk. This is not a hypothetical. When Discord implemented its age verification system in 2025, it suffered a data breach involving more than 70,000 identity documents.

The other option is verification systems based on biometric data, which have shown limitations in practice. In Australia, teenagers have resorted to everything from VPNs, fake birthdates, their parents’ or older siblings’ credentials, and even fake mustaches to maintain access.

Childhood and adolescence are also not universal experiences. Kids in different regions or from different socioeconomic backgrounds use social media dissimilarly. “We cannot—and must not—ignore the fact that conditions regarding access, supervision, and exposure to digital risks are profoundly unequal between countries and even within the same region,” says Dilmar Villena, executive director of Hiperderecho in Peru and a specialist in digital rights, “especially in contexts like Latin America, where gaps in education and access to public services persist.”

Finally, the idea of social media bans also raises issues of consistency. If the goal is to protect children from digital harm, why base such bans on a specific list of platforms when much of that harm is structural and cuts across many online spaces? Applications such as WhatsApp and platforms like Roblox, where minors can interact with strangers, were left out of many of these regulations. However, some include tools with educational potential, such as YouTube. Moreover, a regulation built around a list of applications seems destined to become obsolete.

New technologies always come with new risks, but the goal is not to ban innovation or cut off access for vulnerable groups. It is about seeking to understand how these technologies affect them and responding with proportionate measures to manage those risks.

As Angie Contreras, a representative of Cultivando el Género and a specialist in online child protection, points out, digital life is not a space separate from everyday life, but rather an extension of it. The answer should not be to exclude children and adolescents from digital spaces, but to ensure that platforms are designed to be used safely, with the same protections and rights that minors enjoy offline.

In this sense, social media is more like a bicycle than a cigarette. We know accidents can happen, but no one suggests banning them. We build bike lanes, establish traffic rules, teach people how to use them, promote helmets and knee pads, and demand safety standards. We reduce the risks without giving up their benefits.

What would these solutions look like when applied to the digital world? Perhaps the first step is to include children and adolescents themselves in the debate, Villena suggests. Only by understanding why and how they use these platforms will it be possible to design responses that address the real risks without sacrificing the benefits these spaces also offer.

In particular, each stakeholder has a unique role and responsibility in creating a safer future for minors in digital environments. Governments must develop public policies and regulations based on evidence rather than knee-jerk reactions. Brazil, for example, has demanded the incorporation of more accessible parental controls, a reduction in addictive design practices such as infinite scrolling, greater transparency regarding how its algorithms work, and that major tech companies have specialized staff in the country to ensure accountability.

Platforms must take greater responsibility for the design of their algorithms, as this is one of the main challenges. It is urgent that schools incorporate digital literacy as part of their daily curriculum and design protocols to prevent, detect, and address situations of online violence. Families and caregivers need tools and guidelines to support children in their digital lives.

Both those who support children and those who navigate these spaces need support to know how to respond to risky situations online, says Dafna Viniegra, founder of Infancia Libre de Abuso Sexual. Initiatives such as the VigIAchatbot strengthen these capabilities and provide guidance on the challenges of the digital world for children. Children and adolescents themselves must be considered as rights-holders, not merely as people who need to be protected.

That is the direction new regulatory proposals are beginning to take. Lorena Villavicencio, executive secretary of Mexico’s National System for the Comprehensive Protection of Children and Adolescents, stated that they are preparing an initiative to regulate social media, but that it is not just a matter of controls. “We believe the most important thing is to ensure, from the very design of the platforms, that enhanced protection for children and adolescents is built in,” Villavicencio says.

Perhaps it’s time to stop asking ourselves how to keep children off the internet and start asking why we find it easier to ban things than to demand better conditions.

This article originally appeared on WIRED en Español and has been translated from Spanish.

entries.title: My Week of Transparent Living With Apple’s ‘More Clear’ Liquid Glass Setting
entries.summary: I cranked Apple’s iOS 27 beta to maximum transparency on my iPhone and enjoyed every warped second.
entries.content:
I scroll on my phone, sipping on an overpriced espresso tonic at a San Francisco café. I watch as glassy notifications pop up on my home screen, one after another, distorting the top row of app icons in neat succession. Similar to how the glass in my hand refracts the coffee’s colors, the warping effect of Apple’s Liquid Glass tugs at the edges of whatever's behind each notification, disappearing a second later.

Apple dropped the public beta for iOS 27 in mid-July, with the software’s full release expected in September. The beta version of iOS includes a new slider for adjusting Liquid Glass’s appearance across your iPhone. Apple’s translucent operating system was polarizing when it launched in 2025, with some criticizing how the design impacted text legibility. In response, Apple rolled out updates that tampered with the Liquid Glass effect, making the smartphone interface more opaque.

With iOS 27, Apple gives users the option to make Liquid Glass even more transparent than its default. In the current public beta, this choice is available in Settings under the Appearance tab. If you tap on Liquid Glass here, you can drag it to the left for a more transparent experience.

Fear not—if you don’t feel the same way as I do about transparency—you can drag that same slider to the right and boost the overall opacity of Liquid Glass. There’s still the option to Reduce Transparency and Increase Contrast as well.

I immediately cranked my settings to maximum clarity after I downloaded the public beta and left the setting on for a week to get the full experience. I understand why some prefer the heavily tinted version or need to switch it for accessibility reasons, yet I reveled in the warped colors and characters as I experienced Liquid Glass in its clearest glory.

Early during my tests, I found myself slowly scrolling up and down on my Slack messages, watching as the names of Slack rooms refracted behind the menu bar at the bottom of my smartphone screen. I watched as the letters curled into meaningless lines and then reformed, unharmed, on the other side. The more I stared, the more whimsical it felt, and the more I appreciated Apple’s glassy design risk.

The “more clear” option also added an increased depth to my device—reminiscent of an Apple update from more than a decade ago, in iOS 7, that you likely haven’t thought about in a minute: 3D apps. Apple added a parallax wallpaper effect during the iPhone 5’s heyday that used the smartphone’s gyroscope to make it appear as if app icons on your home screen floated slightly above the background, visibly shifting to the phone’s tilt.

Both Liquid Glass and 3D app icons gave the iPhone a more tactile sense of dimensionality. “Translucency, parallax, animation, and motion (like a weather app showing falling snow) give the system a dive-in quality that it didn't have before. It appears three-dimensional in some ways,” wrote former WIRED staffer Mat Honan about Apple’s announcement of its iOS 7 redesign. I experienced a similar “dive-in quality” when I cranked up the transparency of Liquid Glass in iOS 27.

Halfway through my week of testing, this option started to look so normal to me that I worried I had accidentally reset my phone to the default and checked my settings to ensure “more clear” was still enabled. It was. Which was a relief, and a sign I’d adjusted to this clearer version of Liquid Glass even faster than anticipated.

If anything, the glassy effect became quite easy to ignore most of the time as I used my device. The clearest design elements of Liquid Glass often seemed relegated to menu bars or other smaller details near the edges of my screen. So, the legibility of what I was attempting to read was rarely impacted. Often, I would notice Liquid Glass warping words and colors out of the corner of my eye as I used my iPhone.

This transparency toggle is not as flashy a change as others available in the current iOS 27 beta, most notably the Siri assistant overhaul. Even so, this clear-minded update was delightful to try, and I’m keeping up with my transparent lifestyle even after the weeklong experiment ended. I don’t mind if my smartphone functions more like a beautiful piece of art at times rather than always prioritizing utility—clearly.
